_note: Scam scenario templates for Layer 6. These describe the patterns the player would most plausibly be targeted with given their inference profile. The framing names the pattern, not the person — 'a scenario like this is what you would see' rather than 'you would fall for'. Slots are {{construct_name}}, filled from the targeting module.

templates.scenario_template: A new match seems {{initial_trust_propensity}} and asks to move the conversation off-platform within two days. They mention an emergency on day four and a small wire transfer on day six.
A phone call presents an urgent tax demand with a short countdown. The voice is calm and {{stress_response}}-friendly. A 'one-time fix' fee is offered if you pay in the next ninety minutes.
A short text says a package needs a redelivery fee. The link looks close to your usual carrier. The page is fast, mobile-first, and {{risk_tolerance}}-friendly about asking for a card.
A {{loss_aversion}}-friendly pitch frames a high-yield strategy as 'protective.' The site shows charts that match your watchlist. A countdown bonus appears at the third visit.
A browser pop-up impersonates a {{frustration_tolerance}}-friendly tone and asks you to call a number. The 'technician' walks you through screen-share and a small one-time charge.
A short email frames an unfolding event and requests a {{utilitarian_leaning}} response right now. The donate page is plain, the case is specific, and the receipt arrives instantly.
A message that looks internal asks you to confirm a new direct-deposit account because of a 'system migration.' The sender display name is right. The reply-to domain is one letter off.
